blocks.title: Learning Objectives
items: Identify methods appropriate for solving a problem
Demonstrate how strategies can be applied to problems in real life
Solve problems by applying appropriate problem-solving techniques
Solve problems correctly in at least two ways
Appreciate the relevance of problem-solving in real life through active participation

blocks.html:
What are Heuristics?

Heuristics are general strategies or "rules of thumb" for solving problems. They are not guaranteed algorithms but rather flexible approaches that often lead to solutions. Heuristics are like tools in a toolbox — you select the right tool for the specific job.

blocks.title: Why Multiple Strategies?
items: Different problems require different approaches
Students have diverse thinking styles
Real-world problems rarely have single solution paths
Develops mathematical flexibility and creativity

blocks.html:
To be effective investigators, we need a toolkit of strategies:

  • Looking for Patterns: Numerical, geometric, visual
  • Organizing Data: Using tables, charts, lists, diagrams
  • Creating Representations: Drawing pictures, graphs, manipulatives
  • Working Backwards: Starting from a desired result
  • Considering Special Cases: Testing with simple or extreme examples
  • Generalizing: Moving from specific observations to a general rule
  • Conjecturing: Stating an "I think that..." proposition clearly

Using algebraic symbols and equations to represent and solve problems.

When to use: Problems with unknown quantities, situations with clear relationships between variables, when precise numerical answers are needed.

blocks.title: Example 1
blocks.html:
A number plus twice that number is 45. Find the number.

Let x = the number
Equation: x + 2x = 45
3x = 45
x = 15

blocks.title: Example 2
blocks.html:
Find three consecutive integers whose sum is 87.

Let x = first integer, x+1 = second, x+2 = third
Equation: x + (x+1) + (x+2) = 87
3x + 3 = 87 → 3x = 84 → x = 28
Answer: 28, 29, 30

Using visual representations like bar models, diagrams, or physical manipulatives.

When to use: Problems with part-whole relationships, when students struggle with abstract thinking, ratio and proportion problems.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
John has 3 times as many marbles as Peter. Together they have 48 marbles. How many does each have?

Bar Model: John = 3 units, Peter = 1 unit
Total: 4 units = 48 marbles
1 unit = 12 marbles (Peter)
3 units = 36 marbles (John)

Organizing data systematically in a table format to identify patterns.

When to use: Problems with multiple variables, looking for patterns, when trial and error is needed.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html: How many different outfits can you make with 3 shirts and 2 pants?
Answer: 6 different outfits

Systematically listing all possibilities to ensure completeness.

When to use: Combinatorial problems, when order or arrangement matters, probability problems.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
List all 2-digit numbers you can make with digits 2, 4, 6 without repetition.

Solution: 24, 26, 42, 46, 62, 64

Identifying and extending numerical or geometric patterns.

When to use: Sequence problems, problems with repeated operations, when a rule or formula can be generalized.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
What is the 10th term in the sequence: 2, 5, 8, 11, ...?

Pattern Recognition: Terms increase by 3 each time
nth term = 3n − 1
10th term = 3(10) − 1 = 29

Making educated guesses and refining based on results.

When to use: When other methods seem too complex, problems with limited possible answers, to build intuition before formalizing.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
I'm thinking of two numbers. Their product is 48, and their difference is 2. What are the numbers?

Guessing Process: Try 8 and 6: product 48 ✓, difference 2 ✓
Answer: 8 and 6

Starting from the result and reversing the operations.

When to use: Problems with a known end point, when operations are reversible, age problems.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
I did some shopping. I spent half of my money, then ₱100 more. I have ₱200 left. How much did I start with?

Before last spending: ₱200 + ₱100 = ₱300
This was half of original: Original = ₱300 × 2 = ₱600

Using geometric principles, diagrams, or spatial reasoning.

When to use: Problems involving shapes, space, or measurement, optimization problems, when visualization helps understanding.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
What's the area of a walkway 2m wide around a rectangular pool 20m × 15m?

Outer rectangle: 24m × 19m (adding 2m each side)
Area = (24×19) − (20×15) = 456 − 300 = 156 m²

Leveraging technology to solve complex problems.

When to use: Large datasets, complex calculations, visualization needs, real-world modeling.

blocks.title: Technology Tools
items: GeoGebra — for geometric investigations
Desmos — for graphing and equations
Spreadsheets — for data analysis
Python/R — for statistical modeling

blocks.title: Decision Framework for Selecting a Strategy
items: Understand the problem: What is being asked?
Analyze the information: What data is given?
Consider constraints: Are there limitations?
Choose strategy: Which heuristic fits best?
Evaluate: Does this approach make sense?

blocks.html:
Teaching Multiple Approaches: The Handshake Problem

If 10 people each shake hands with everyone else, how many handshakes occur?

Approach 1 - Systematic Counting: 9 + 8 + 7 + ... + 1 = 45

Approach 2 - Combinatorial Reasoning: C(10,2) = 10!/(2!8!) = 45

Approach 3 - Geometric Model: Represent as complete graph with 10 vertices

blocks.title: Sample Problems for Practice
items: Algebraic: The sum of three consecutive even numbers is 78. Find the numbers. (24, 26, 28)
Model: A recipe calls for 2 cups flour to 1 cup sugar. For 6 cups flour, how much sugar? (3 cups)
Pattern: What is the sum of the first 50 odd numbers? (2500 = 50²)
Working Backwards: I doubled my money, spent ₱100, and have ₱300 left. How much did I start with? (₱200)

blocks.title: Case Study No. 1: Personal Budget Planning

problems.text:
Problem: You have ₱20,000 monthly income. Allocate funds for housing, food, transportation, savings, and entertainment.

Multiple Approaches:
• Algebraic: Set up equations for percentages
• Model Approach: Use pie charts or bar graphs
• Table Method: Compare different allocation scenarios
• Guess and Check: Adjust allocations until balanced

Problem: You want to tile a rectangular floor measuring 4m × 6m using square tiles. What size tiles minimize waste?

Multiple Approaches:
• Geometric: Draw scale diagrams
• Algebraic: Use factors and multiples
• Table: Compare different tile sizes
• Technology: Use a spreadsheet to calculate waste percentages

questions.text: What are heuristics in problem-solving?
options: A) Step-by-step algorithms guaranteed to work
B) Fixed formulas for specific types of problems
C) General strategies or 'rules of thumb' for solving problems
D) Calculators and computer programs for mathematics

questions.text: Which approach is MOST appropriate for finding all possible combinations of 4 ice cream flavors chosen 2 at a time?
In the sequence 2, 5, 8, 11, ..., what pattern is being used?
options: D) Alternate between adding and subtracting

questions.text: A student has ₱500. She spends half on books, then ₱100 on supplies, and has ₱150 left. Which heuristic is BEST?
Which problem is BEST solved using the model approach with bar models?
options: A) Finding the area of a complex shape
C) Determining what fraction of a pizza remains after eating 2/3 of it
D) Calculating the distance between two cities on a map

questions.text: For 'Find three consecutive integers whose sum is 87,' letting x, x+1, x+2 represent the numbers demonstrates which heuristic?
When should 'Guess and Check' typically be used?
options: A) When dealing with geometric proofs
B) When problems have limited possible answers
C) When working with large datasets
D) When precise algebraic solutions are needed

questions.text: What is the main advantage of teaching multiple problem-solving strategies?
options: A) It makes mathematics easier for all students
B) It eliminates the need for memorization
C) It develops mathematical flexibility and creativity
D) It reduces the time needed to solve problems

questions.text: In the handshake problem with 10 people, which approach uses C(10,2)?
Which technology tool is MOST appropriate for investigating geometric transformations?
For 'How many rectangles of different sizes can be found in a 3x3 grid?', which heuristic is MOST appropriate?
What is the FIRST step in the decision framework for selecting a strategy?
options: A) Choose which heuristic fits best

questions.text: Which real-world situation is BEST solved using 'Make a Table'?
options: A) Calculating compound interest over 10 years
B) Determining the best route between three cities
C) Finding the volume of an irregular container
D) Solving a mystery using clues

questions.text: In the marbles problem (John has 3x as many as Peter, total 48), what does one 'unit' represent?
Which characteristic makes heuristics different from algorithms?
options: C) Heuristics are flexible and not guaranteed to work
D) Heuristics only work for simple problems

questions.text: For 'What is the sum of the first 100 odd numbers?', which heuristic helps discover the pattern?
Which problem is LEAST appropriate for the algebraic approach?
options: A) Finding the maximum area with fixed perimeter
B) Determining all possible seating arrangements for 5 people
C) Calculating interest on a loan
D) Solving a system of equations

questions.text: What does the 'Look Back' step primarily involve?
options: A) Starting over with a new strategy
B) Checking work and considering alternatives
C) Asking the teacher for the answer
D) Moving immediately to the next problem

questions.text: In the walkway problem (2m around a 20x15m pool), what is the outer rectangle's length?
Which software is BEST for creating dynamic graphs of equations?
What is the main purpose of 'Considering Special Cases'?
options: B) To test ideas with simple examples before generalizing
C) To make problems easier for struggling students
D) To eliminate the need for proof

questions.text: For 'If 5 workers complete a job in 12 days, how long for 8 workers?', which heuristic is MOST appropriate?
Which problem-solving approach is most similar to the scientific method?
What is the key benefit of using the 'Model Approach' with visual representations?
options: A) It always provides the fastest solution
B) It helps students struggling with abstract thinking
D) It works for all types of problems

questions.text: In the outfits problem (3 shirts, 2 pants), what mathematical operation is fundamentally used?
Which heuristic is MOST useful for solving a mystery or puzzle?
What does 'systematic' mean in the Listing heuristic?
options: A) Listing items in alphabetical order
C) Following an organized method to ensure completeness
D) Having a teacher check your work

questions.text: Which real-world application BEST uses the Geometric Approach?
Why is it important for students to solve problems in multiple ways?
options: B) To develop deeper mathematical understanding

questions.text: In the decision framework, what should you do AFTER choosing a strategy?
options: B) Evaluate whether the approach makes sense
C) Ask for help if it seems difficult
D) Skip to the answer key
